coredata.dc:title: Red-emitting carbon dots as luminescent agent in wide-range water detection in organic solvents and polarity-selective zebrafish imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Weiying
Liu, Chuang
Fan, Jiaqi
Luo, Jiabao
Liu, Lihong
Huang, Jinqing
Liu, Ruiyuan
Zhang, Xinguo
coredata.dc:description: Heteroatom-doped carbon dots with promising luminescent properties are excellent candidates for sensing and imaging applications. Herein, we reported a facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis of highly-fluorescent red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) with quantum yield of 37.2 %. R-CDs exhibit a maximum emission band at 640 nm under the excitation wavelength of 500 nm. Furthermore, R-CDs show excitation-dependent and solvent-engineered fluorescent characteristics that the luminescent properties could be greatly affected by polarity. Due to the sensitivity to polarity, a quick and precise approach for wide-range water detection in organic solvents has been developed. Because of the low autofluorescence background, R-CDs were applied to the zebrafish larvae imaging. Polarity-selective bright red light from R-CDs is seen in the low-polarity organs like yolk sac and eyes, which contain abundant lipids and lipoprotein. Our results demonstrate that R-CDs could be used as bio-imaging agents and fluorescent sensors for quantitative and visual water detection in various organic solvents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Red emission
Polarity sensing
Water detection
Bio-imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: QY and lifetime measurements of R-CDs
Detection of water in organic solvents
Detection of water content in organic solvents
Bio-imaging of R-CDs in zebrafish larvae

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) (B) TEM image and the size distribution (insert) of R-CD. (C) FTIR spectrum and (D) the wide scan XPS spectrum of R-CDs. High resolution XPS of (E) B 1s, (F) C 1s, (G) N 1s and (H) O 1s spectra of R-CDs.

(A) Excitation and emission spectra and the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of R-CDs. The photographs under sunlight and excitation at 500 nm. (B) The lifetime value and exponential fit of R-CDs. (C) Emission spectra of R-CDs at different concentrations with the corresponding photographs. (D) PL spectra of R-CDs excited at different wavelengths.

(A) Emission spectra of R-CDs with different solvents and (B) linear relationship of relative fluorescence intensity of R-CDs with polarizability. (C) Emission spectra of R-CDs at different water contents. (D) Relationship of relative fluorescence intensities with water content in water/ THF system and (E) the corresponding photographs at 500 nm excitation.
The fitting results of R-CDs for the water contents in different solvents. Fitting formula: y = a + bx.
(a) Bright field and (b) fluorescence field images were used to observe zebrafish larvae (72 hpf) incubated with R-CDs for different time under different incubation concentrations.
Solvents and parameters of R-CDs in different solvents.
The performances of R-CDs used for water content detections in organic solvents.
Application of the proposed method for determination of water content in H2O/THF system, the found was the value of four parallel experiments.

head.ce:title: Red-emitting carbon dots as luminescent agent in wide-range water detection in organic solvents and polarity-selective zebrafish imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NMPA Key Laboratory for Research and Evaluation of Drug Metabolism, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou 510515, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: NMPA Key Laboratory for Research and Evaluation of Drug Metabolism, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southern Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: NMPA Key Laboratory for Research and Evaluation of Drug Metabolism, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, 510515, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Medical Image Processing, School of Biomedical Engineering, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou 510515, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Medical Image Processing, School of Biomedical Engineering, Southern Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Medical Image Processing, School of Biomedical Engineering, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou 510515, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, Clear Water Bay, Kowloon, Hong Kong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, Clear Water Bay, Kowloon, Hong Kong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heteroatom-doped carbon dots with promising luminescent properties are excellent candidates for sensing and imaging applications. Herein, we reported a facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis of highly-fluorescent red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) with quantum yield of 37.2 %. R-CDs exhibit a maximum emission band at 640 nm under the excitation wavelength of 500 nm. Furthermore, R-CDs show excitation-dependent and solvent-engineered fluorescent characteristics that the luminescent properties could be greatly affected by polarity. Due to the sensitivity to polarity, a quick and precise approach for wide-range water detection in organic solvents has been developed. Because of the low autofluorescence background, R-CDs were applied to the zebrafish larvae imaging. Polarity-selective bright red light from R-CDs is seen in the low-polarity organs like yolk sac and eyes, which contain abundant lipids and lipoprotein. Our results demonstrate that R-CDs could be used as bio-imaging agents and fluorescent sensors for quantitative and visual water detection in various organic solvents.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as novel carbon-based nanomaterials, have merits of narrow and tunable emission peaks, high quantum yield (QY), good biocompatibility, multitudinous applications and low cost. Most of reported CDs show high QY in blue and green spectral regions, but these short-wavelength emissions are easily absorbed by the biological tissue and disturbed by background interference, therefore their practical applications for bio-sensing and bio-imaging are strictly limited. Long-wavelength-emitting (λ em>600 nm) CDs have advantages of deep penetration and low radiation damage. However, high-efficient red/infrared-emitting CDs are relatively lacked and the exploration of them is a major challenge in further CDs development [1–3]. Currently, heteroatomic doping is known as one of effective strategies to induce emission enhancement and red-shift of emission peak [4,5]. Several CDs with excellent luminescence, negligible cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility are reported as fluorescent agents for sensing and imaging [6–10].

Polarity, as an important parameter, could affect numerous organic and inorganic processes [11]. It displays an essential role in the interaction of proteins and enzymes in biological systems [12,13]. Water content control is crucial for organic reactions, pharmaceutical production and food storage. Water impurity can cause undesirable reactions, lower production yield or even result in explosions [14]. Karl-Fischer titration and chromatography are standard analytical methods for water detection, which are expensive, complicated and require skillful operators. Developing a convenient, quick and precise water content detection probe is of great need. In recent years, fluorescent sensing technology has been widely concerned. There are a lot of attempts to develop fluorescent probes with polarity sensing and then are applied on the detection of water. It has been reported that the luminescence of CDs has a quick and obvious response with changing solvent polarity [15–17]. Owing to simplicity, sensitivity, high reproducibility and fast response, CDs-based luminescent sensor is of great potential in field of detecting polarity and water content in organic solvents [14,18].

Herein, we synthesized red-emitting CDs (R-CDs) with heteroatomic (B3+) doping by using dimethylaminobenzaldehyde (DMAB) and boric acid (H3BO3) as novel precursors successfully. R-CDs exhibit a maximum emission band at 640 nm with the best excitation wavelength of 500 nm, having a QY of 37.2 % in Tetrahydrofuran (THF). It is found that R-CDs show solvent-engineered fluorescent performance that the luminescence could be greatly affected by polarity. Thereafter, the R-CDs are used as fluorescence sensors for water detection in organic solvents including THF, 1,4-dioxane, acetone, N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol and methanol. The good sensing performances with excellent linear fitting (R
2 >0.99), broad detection range of 0–70 % (v/v) and low detection limits (0.331–1.58 %) are demonstrated. Because of the low autofluorescence background, negligible-cytotoxic and good-biocompatible, R-CDs were applied to the zebrafish larvae imaging. Polarity-selective bright red light from R-CDs could be clearly seen in the yolk sac and eyes owing to the abundance of lipids and lipoprotein, which are of low polarity. Based on this discovery, the as-prepared R-CDs could be explored as visual fluorescent sensors for bio-imaging and realizing real-time quantitative detection of water content in different kinds of organic solvents.

R-CDs were prepared with 0.0746 g DMAB and 0.0309 g H3BO3 in 5 mL ethanol dispersed by sonicating for 5 min, transferred into a 40 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After a 0.22 µm membrane was used for filtration to remove impurities, R-CDs exhibiting outstanding solubility in ethanol were obtained without any complicated purification. And a light brown power was obtained after removing the solvent of R-CDs by reduced pressure distillation.

Dimethylaminobenzaldehyde (DMAB) was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. Boric acid (H3BO3) was purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory. Different solvents were bought from Guangzhou GHTECH Co. Ltd. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements, Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectra and lifetime were recorded by a Tecnai F20 microscope, a ESCALAB 250Xi and Metash UV-5200 spectrophotometer, Edinburgh FLS980 spectrometer, respectively. Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded Bruker/TENSOR Ⅱ ranging from 400 to 4000 cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: QY and lifetime measurements of R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The QY of R-CDs was measured, and Rhodamine B (RhB) (relative QY 56 %, ethanol as solvent) was chosen as standards. The absorbance of all samples was kept below 0.10 to minimize the re-absorption effects. Then, the fluorescence emission was obtained at the excitation of 500 nm. QY was calculated by using the equation:
Q =
Q
R
I
I
R
A
R
A
n
2
n
R
2
Where Q is the QY, I is the integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index, and A represents the optical density. R represents to the standard.

Decay curve can be explored and well fitted to a bi-exponential decay model (Fig. 2E):
I
( t )
=
I
0
+
A
1
exp ( −
t
τ
1
) +
A
2
exp ( −
t
τ
2
)
Where I is the luminescence intensity; τ
1 and τ
2 are the life components in bi-exponential decay model; A
1 and A
2 are constants; t is the time. And decay lifetime values were calculated by:
τ
a v g
=
A
1
τ
1
2
+
A
2
τ
2
2
A
1
τ
1
+
A
2
τ
2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water in organic solvents

ce:section.ce:para: One hour after the female spawning, the transparent embryos were taken out and placed in a petri dish filled with embryo culture solution. The temperature was maintained at 28 °C ± 0.5 °C. The larvae with normal development were selected for the experiment. All the animal procedures were performed in accordance with the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of Southern Medical University and approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Southern Medical University.

The morphology of as-prepared R-CDs was determined by TEM and shown in Fig. 1A, R-CDs were observed to be well-dispersed nano-particles with a Gaussian particle size about 5.0 nm. High-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1B) shows that R-CDs have a clear lattice fringe around 0.18 nm which is close to 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) planes of graphite. To further investigate surface composition of R-CDs, FTIR spectroscopy and XPS analysis were performed. FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1C) shows broad absorption peak at 3215.03 cm−1, which is assigned to OH/BO-H bonds [19]. Three absorption peaks at 3000–2700 cm−1 are owing to the hydrophobic alkyl chains and aromatic rings. In addition, peak appears at 1598.21 cm−1, which is the tensile vibration of CC in the aromatic ring skeleton. An absorption peak at 1440.82 cm−1 is owing to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of B-O/B-H [20,21]. The band at 1163.25 cm−1 is the typical tensile vibration mode of CN/C−N heterocycles. The absorption peak at 805 cm−1 is assigned to -CH2 rocking vibrations. The abundant N/O substructures on the surface give R-CDs excellent solubility in a variety of solvents. As can be seen from XPS full spectrum and element content statistics (Fig. 1D), the peaks of B 1s (193.08 eV), C 1s (287.0 eV), N 1s (401.3 eV) and O 1s (531.0 eV) appear in R-CDs, indicating the existence of B, C, N and O elements. The forms of these elements are further studied by analyzing high resolution XPS spectra. The B 1s spectrum shows two peaks at 193.4 eV and 192.4 eV, which are assigned to B-O and B-N, respectively (Fig. 1E). The C 1s spectrum can be resolved into three peaks indicating the presence of C-N/C-O (286.8 eV), C-C/CC (285.5 eV) and C-B (284.4 eV) (Fig. 1F). The N 1s spectrum includes three dominant peaks at 405.7 eV, 400.6 eV and 399.5 eV, assigned to N-H, C-N, and amino N, respectively (Fig. 1G). The O 1s spectrum involves peaks of C-O (533.2 eV) and CO (532.2 eV) (Fig. 1H). Both FT-IR and XPS reveal that abundant functional groups remain on R-CDs surface, such as boron, oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups like CN/C−N and OH/BO-H.

The luminescent properties of R-CDs were characterized by PL/PLE/Decay and UV–vis absorption. Fig. 2A shows the representative absorption, excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs in ethanol solution, corresponding to blue, green and red lines, respectively. As for the UV–vis absorption, the peaks at around 251 nm and 516 nm are corresponding to π–π * transitions of the sp2-hybridized core and n–π * transition, respectively. Upon 500 nm excitation, R-CDs emit a bright red emission at 640 nm, which might be assigned to surface-state emission due to its excitation-dependent property (Fig. 2D). Furthermore, TCSPC experiment shows (Fig. 2B) that the average fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs is evaluated as 3.39 ns. When the concentration increases from 1 mg/mL to 15 mg/mL, a small red shift of the peak wavelength from 635 nm to 641 nm is observed (Fig. 2C), which is due to the energy gap shrinkage by R-CDs aggregation at high concentrations. The graphs of R-CDs ethanol solutions at different concentrations clearly exhibit the similar tendency at 500 nm excitation (Fig. 2C inset). In addition, the emission wavelength of R-CDs is dependent of excitation wavelength from 440 to 580 nm with a gradual red shift from 618 to 646 nm (Fig. 2D), which is attributed to the various surface states of R-CDs [18,19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para:
More interestingly, beside the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, solvent-dependent performances of CDs were observed in solvents with diverse polarity, which have been widely reported [15–17]. R-CDs show solvent-engineered fluorescent characteristics that luminescence varies with the change of solvent ( Fig. 3A). Tunable PL emissions are attributed to the prior interaction between the solvent molecules or the existence of surface trap states, which leads to the difference of energy levels in various solvents [22]. In order to effectively investigate the effect of polarity, we introduced a polarity parameter
E
N
T
, which describes the solvation capability of solvents [23]. As shown in Table 1 and Fig. 3B, the relative intensity exhibits a linear dependence (R
2 = 0.933) with solvent polarity
E
N
T
. As
E
N
T
increases, the decrease of relative fluorescence intensity from 100 % to 0.993 % is observed. Also, the emission spectra of R-CDs in different solvents present the same trends (Fig. 3A), which can be explained by the formation of hydrogen bonds between R-CDs and solvents that stabilize the energy level of the excited state [24]. These optical characterizations suggest that R-CDs can be used as potential sensors for the polarity.

Measuring water content in organic solvents has been a hotspot of research in the chemical industry. However, Karl Fischer titration widely used in the past years is limited seriously by the need of instruments and expertise [25]. Therefore, CDs-based water detection in organic solvents is widely used as a sensitive, simple and fast-responded approach [26,27]. In this case, fluorescent detection of water content is realized by the luminescent response of R-CDs in various solvents. As a representative, the relationship between fluorescence intensity of R-CDs and the water content of H2O/THF system was explored (Fig. 3C). Both the decrease of relative emission intensity from 100 % to 3.90 % and the red shift of maximum emission peak from 609 nm to 653 nm are observed when the water content increases from 0 % to 80 %. The relative fluorescence intensity has a wide detection ranging from 20 % to 80 %, described by the Stern-Volmer equation: y = −0.00372 x + 0.995, R
2 = 0.997, where y is I/I
0 . An obvious color change from a bright red to a dark red with the increase of the polarity of solvent under 500 nm shown in Fig. 3E, could be clearly seen by naked eyes.

The quenching of fluorescence may result from the internal conversion and vibrational relaxation, which could increase strength of the hydrogen bonding formed between oxygen-related groups and water molecules and compete with fluorescence, which is supposed to be caused by the polarity-induced dissolution of the aggregate. The fluorescence ion structure is destroyed by water and the surface carboxyl group is hydrolyzed. The increasing number of proton transfer decreases the fluorescence significantly when water ion concentration increases [28]. And the red shift of fluorescence emission is because the energy of reorienting the molecular dipoles have changed and excited electrons have lost in higher polarity solvents [29].

Inspired by this phenomenon, we further applied R-CDs for detecting water content in other organic solvents including 1,4-dioxane, acetone, DMF, DMSO, methanol and ethanol. Fig. 4 plots the relationships between the relative emission intensity of R-CDs and the water content in different water/organic solvents system. As water content in solvents increases, fluorescence intensity of R-CDs decreases. The relations between the relative intensity and water content exhibit great linearity, repeatability, a low detection limit that R
2 ranges from 0.916 to 0.999, LOD ranges from 0.331 % to 1.58 % and a wide detection range, especially in water/ethanol system (0–70 %) showed in Table 2. To our knowledge, several typically beneficial attempts have been tried in publications. Huo et al. [24] developed a nanoprobe exhibiting detection performance from 0 % to 20 % and 60–100 %, and LOD ranges from 0.0460 to 1.25 vol% in six different solvents. Wang et al. [30] developed carbon dots-based test strip detecting the water content in the range of 0–20 % for ethanol and 0–8 % for acetone. LOD ranges from 0.011 % to 0.071 %. Kumari et al. [31] developed CDs-based water detection sensor to detect water content from 0.0 % to 0.8 %. LOD is from 0.037 % to 0.072 % in three different solvents. Chen [32] et al. had synthesized fluorescent sensors for the qualitative detection (0–20 %) of low-level water content in THF or dioxane. Compared with above-mentioned results, the R-CDs possess wider detection range in more solvents systems: THF (20–80 %), 1,4-dioxane (0–10/10–70 %), acetone (30–80 %), DMF (0–10/20–80 %), DMSO (20–40/40–80 %), methanol (10–30/30–70 %) and ethanol (0–70 %).

In order to verify the feasibility and accuracy of fluorescence probes based on R-CDs for detecting water content in practical application, the experiments had been designed to detect water content in H2O/THF system. The operating steps were conducted in the same conditions and the results were presented in Table 3. It could be seen that the recovery rises from 94.2 % to 116 % and RSD ranges from 0.0309 % to 0.467 %. The results indicate that the fluorescence probes have good accuracy, repeatability and precision. Thus, it’s feasible to detect water content in real THF samples. All above results reveal that our R-CDs is the potential fluorescent sensors for water detection in various solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-imaging of R-CDs in zebrafish larvae
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the rapid growth and optical transparency, zebrafish have widely used in imaging. When zebrafish embryos develop to 72 h past fertilization (hpf), the chorionic membrane falls off naturally. Then, zebrafish larvae could contact with water directly, and CDs can enter the body through transdermal absorption, respiration and swallowing. In conclusion, more absorption ways make zebrafish larvae more suitable than embryos to treat with R-CDs. Hence, zebrafish larvae were used in imaging. However, most of reported CDs-based bio-imaging agents are showing blue to orange fluorescence with low penetration depth, while bright red-emitting CDs, which could realize clear, deep-tissue and real-time imaging, are relatively lacked and thus in great need [33–35].

Bio-imaging was implemented by incubating zebrafish larvae with R-CDs. As can be seen from Fig. 5, the control groups have no fluorescence under 500 nm excitation. However, zebrafish larvae with R-CDs show a clear red fluorescence, suggesting that R-CDs have entered the body of fish. With the increase of R-CDs concentration and incubation time, the fluorescence gradually increases. R-CDs show polarity-dependent fluorescent characteristics with weak luminescence in high polarity environment, while strong red fluorescence in low polarity system. Bright red light from R-CDs could be clearly seen in the yolk sac and eyes owing to the abundance of lipids and lipoprotein, which are of low polarity. Thus, polarity-selective imaging in zebrafish could be realized. When R-CDs concentration is 1.00 mg/mL and incubation time is 4 h, the fluorescence reaches the maximum. The viability of zebrafish (72 hpf) treated with various concentration of R-CDs for 4 h was also examined. Even when R-CDs concentration is up to 1.00 mg/mL, the zebrafish could still survive with good health status and no observed developmental damage (viability>75 %), indicating R-CDs can be used in polarity bio-imaging specifically because of the negligible cytotoxicity in living systems.

Herein, the R-CDs were synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal reaction with raw materials of DMAB and H3BO3. R-CDs possess high QY of 37.2 %. The fluorescence spectra show that the optimum excitation wavelength was 500 nm，and the maximum emission wavelength was 640 nm. The luminescence of R-CDs in water/organic solvent system is affected by solvent polarity. The polarity response was then used for detecting water content in organic solvents including THF, 1,4-dioxane, acetone, DMF, DMSO, methanol and ethanol. The relations between the relative intensity and water content exhibit great linearity, repeatability, a low detection limit that R
2 ranges from 0.916 to 0.999, LOD ranges from 0.331 % to 1.58 % and a wide detection range, especially in water/ethanol system (0–70 %). Because of the low autofluorescence background, negligible-cytotoxic and good-biocompatible, R-CDs were applied to the zebrafish larvae imaging. Polarity-selective bright red light from R-CDs is seen in the low-polarity organs like yolk sac and eyes, which contain abundant lipids and lipoprotein. This study provides the promising luminescent agents for zebrafish larvae imaging and real-time monitoring water content.

Weiying Zhou: Writing – original draft. Chuang Liu: Investigation. Jiaqi Fan: Formal analysis. Jiabao Luo: Methodology. Lihong Liu: Formal analysis. Jinqing Huang: Methodology. Ruiyuan Liu: Data curation, Visualization. Xinguo Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was funded by Guangzhou Scientific Planning Program (No. 201804010260), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601081), GuangDong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2018A030313843).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The effect of solvent polarity on emission properties of carbon dots and their uses in colorimetric sensors for water and humidity
